meta.title: Songhae Park (Okyeonji) | Dalseong-gun, Daegu, Korea · Lakeside Eco Park, Song Hae Trail & Four-Season Flower Fields
meta.description: The complete guide to Songhae Park (Okyeonji, Korean: 송해공원 / 옥연지 송해공원): a lakeside eco park on the shore of Okyeonji Pond in Dalseong-gun, Daegu, Korea. A roughly 3.5 km 'Song Hae Trail' (송해둘레길) loops the pond, linking lake views, four-season flower fields (spring azaleas, autumn cosmos and pink muhly), a memorial statue of Song Hae, and photo spots — all free and open 24 hours. Named after Korea's beloved national MC Song Hae (송해, 1926–2022), it is the green living room of Dalseong-gun for strolls, family outings, and quiet escapes. Hours, transport, history, photo spots, and visitor reviews all in one place.

nonprofitBanner.text: This is a non-profit independent guide put together by enthusiastic travelers, offering the most objective information on Songhae Park free of charge, and affiliated with no organization.
nonprofitBanner.dataSource: All information on this site is cross-checked against public materials from Dalseong-gun, the Daegu Metropolitan Government, and the Korea Tourism Organization (VisitKorea), with no commercial endorsements.

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.text: So that every traveler who comes to Daegu won't miss the lake and flower fields on the shore of Okyeonji, we have gathered scattered official information into this guide — to give you the purest experience of 'by the lake, among the flowers, reading a life'.

hero.title: Songhae Park (Okyeonji · 송해공원)
hero.subtitle: A lakeside eco park on the shore of Okyeonji Pond (옥연지) in Dalseong-gun, Daegu, Korea. The roughly 3.5 km 'Song Hae Trail' (송해둘레길) loops the pond, linking lake views, four-season flower fields, a memorial statue of Song Hae, and photo spots. The name 'Okyeonji' is said to mean 'a deep, jade-like pond,' and the park is named after Korea's beloved national MC Song Hae (송해, 1926–2022), carrying the songs and smiles he shared with Koreans for decades. The park is free and open all day.

legend.quote: Songhae Park is named after Korea's beloved national MC Song Hae (송해, 1926–2022). He devoted his life to the National Singing Contest (전국노래자랑), and left the waters and greenery of Okyeonji to the citizens of Dalseong-gun and to travelers from afar.
legend.attribution: — Honorary Citizen of Dalseong-gun · Song Hae (송해)

factsSource.label: Hours & admission per Dalseong County official info

sunsetPlanner.title: Based on today's sunset, we recommend arriving about 60 minutes earlier to catch the softest diffuse light and the blue hour on the water — ideal for the trail, flower fields, and the Song Hae statue.
sunsetPlanner.note: The park wraps around the lake and is open; light is warmest from morning to dusk. On weekends or clear days, allow extra time to avoid crowds.
sunsetPlanner.seaGlare: Sunrise tip: the park embraces Okyeonji Pond and is a popular spot for lake sunrise and morning mist. The first light on the water and flowers is the golden window for photography; mornings are cold in autumn and winter, so dress warmly against the lake breeze.

quickFactsDashboard.title: Songhae Park at a Glance · Data Board
quickFactsDashboard.subtitle: A few numbers to understand this lakeside park on the shore of Okyeonji.

items.label: Okyeonji Pond / Pond
items.note: A natural lakeside green space centered on Okyeonji Pond (옥연지); boardwalks and lawns weave together as Dalseong-gun's most recognizable waterside space.

items.label: Flower Fields / Flowers
items.note: Spring brings azaleas (진달래) and rapeseed; autumn brings cosmos (코스모스) and pink muhly (핑크뮬리). Every season has something in bloom.

items.label: Song Hae Trail / Trail
items.note: A lakeside loop trail (송해둘레길) built along the water — flat and easy, connecting the statue, flower fields, wetlands, and photo spots for the whole family.

items.label: Memorial Statue / Statue
items.note: A memorial statue and photo zone for Song Hae (송해) honors Korea's national MC and is the park's central photo landmark.

items.label: Coordinates / Coords
items.note: Plus Code: QFCM+QP Daegu. Address: 306 Gise-ri, Okpo-eup, Dalseong-gun, Daegu, Korea.

items.label: Admission / Admission
items.note: The park is free and open 24 hours, managed by Dalseong-gun, and can be visited anytime — perfect for walks, families, and photography.

hiddenRules.title: Lakeside Park Visiting Tips
hiddenRules.subtitle: Songhae Park is by the water, open, and full of flower fields. Sort out safety, timing, and budget first, and the experience upgrades from 'rushed check-in' to 'relaxed visit'.

highlights.label: Footwear
highlights.note: Boardwalks and lawns get slippery when wet; wear non-slip shoes, avoid heels; the lake breeze and dew are strong — watch children and seniors.

highlights.label: By the water
highlights.note: Beyond the lakefront and boardwalk railings is Okyeonji Pond. Don't lean on or climb over them; keep a safe distance from the edge when taking photos, and follow on-site guidance.

highlights.label: Weather & Routine
highlights.note: The lakeside is windy, hot in summer and cold in winter; carry a windbreaker and water. On rainy days boardwalks are slippery — wear non-slip shoes.

panels.q: Is there an entrance fee for Songhae Park?
panels.a: The park itself is free and open 24 hours, with no ticket or reservation needed. Some parking lots may charge — please follow on-site signs.
items: The park area is free and accessible anytime.
Most parking is paid public parking; arrive early for a spot.

panels.q: Is it convenient with kids or seniors?
panels.a: The lakeside trail (송해둘레길) is flat and easy; wheelchairs and strollers can reach most viewpoints via the main path. But the lake side is windy and boardwalks can be slippery — hold children, assist seniors, and avoid windy periods.

panels.q: Can I still go on a rainy day?
panels.a: Light rain is fine, but boardwalks are slippery and the lake breeze is strong — wear non-slip shoes and hold the railings. Follow on-site closure guidance during strong winds or maintenance.

complianceGuide.title: Songhae Park · Etiquette & Public-Environment Guide
complianceGuide.subtitle: This is both a visitor's lakeside park and the green living room where Dalseong-gun residents walk and exercise. Following these rules is double respect for nature and for others.

rules.title: Take your trash with you
rules.text: The park is by the water with limited bins; bring a small trash bag and take everything with you when you leave — especially cigarette butts, plastics, and food scraps — keeping lawns and boardwalks clean.

rules.title: Quiet & courtesy, avoid peak times
rules.text: The lakeside is open with clear echoes; lower your voice, don't play music aloud, and leave space for those taking photos and pausing to enjoy the view.

rules.title: No smoking & fire safety
rules.text: Lawns and some covered walkways are no-smoking zones; observe no-smoking and fire-safety signs, and don't smoke among wooden structures or crowds.

rules.title: Protect flower fields & lake ecology
rules.text: The four-season flower fields and Okyeonji Pond are public natural assets — don't step on flower beds, pick plants, or throw things into the lake, keeping this waterside scenery safe and alive.

higashiyamaRoute.title: Custom Itinerary: Songhae Park Half-Day Route
higashiyamaRoute.subtitle: Not just 'who it's for,' but a walkable half-day route you can follow directly. Centered on the Okyeonji loop and flower fields, linking the statue, wetlands, and photo spots.

steps.title: [Start] Park entrance & Okyeonji shore
steps.text: From the entrance, stroll the lakeside trail slowly; lake breeze and waterbirds set the rhythm before you head to the flower fields and statue.
tips: Morning and dusk have the best light and fewer crowds.

steps.title: [Main] Song Hae Trail loop
steps.text: Walk the ~3.5 km lakeside loop (송해둘레길); beneath your feet are the ripples of Okyeonji, connecting flower fields and wetlands. This is the park's most expressive walk and the best vantage for understanding 'lake + flowers'.
tips: The trail is flat and free; allow time for photos and pauses.

steps.title: [Extend] Four-season flower fields & photo spots
steps.text: Wander the azalea beds, cosmos, and pink muhly zones; read the signs to understand the park's blooming calendar and collect nature and memory together.
tips: Flower beds are soft; stay on hard ground with company.

steps.title: [Refuel] Rest area & light meals
steps.text: Hydrate at the rest area and nearby snacks, then look back at the statue and lake horizon, packing lake, flowers, and greenery into one walk.
tips: Supplies are limited; bring your own water.

steps.title: [End] Song Hae statue or extend to Okpo
steps.text: If energy allows, photograph the Song Hae memorial statue, or extend to the nearby Okpo-eup neighborhood; otherwise return along the trail, completing the 'lake + flowers + statue + greenery' half-day package.
tips: Statue light is best after morning; save photos for the end.

higashiyamaRoute.disclaimer: The route above emphasizes a self-contained loop you can follow as-is. If you only want the flowers, keep the first two segments and treat the statue and rest as optional add-ons.

intro.title: Getting to Know Songhae Park
intro.description: Songhae Park (Okyeonji, Korean: 송해공원 / 옥연지 송해공원) sits on the shore of Okyeonji Pond (옥연지) in Dalseong-gun, Daegu, Korea — a waterside eco park built around the lake. A roughly 3.5 km 'Song Hae Trail' (송해둘레길) loops the pond, linking lake views, four-season flower fields, a memorial statue of Song Hae, and photo spots, free and open 24 hours. It is named after Korea's beloved national MC Song Hae (송해, 1926–2022), who hosted the National Singing Contest (전국노래자랑) for decades and is the 'national grandfather' of generations of Koreans. Wrapping around the lake and serenely open, it is Dalseong-gun's green living room — best for slow walks, families, and photography.

visitGuide.title: Visitor Guide
visitGuide.description: Songhae Park is a free lakeside park, open 24 hours with no gate. The ideal approach is rarely 'just wander by day' but to reverse-plan around the light: come at dawn for the softest light on the lake and flowers, or keep the statue and wetlands to yourself on a weekday. Facilities are restrained and commercial feel is low — which is exactly its charm.
items: Dawn or dusk first: lakeside light is softest, and flowers with the statue frame best.
Best views are along the middle of the loop trail and the lakefront railings.
From central Daegu, take the metro to Okpo (옥포), then a bus or taxi about 10–20 min to the entrance.
There is little commercial supply inside; water, restrooms, and breaks are best done at the entrance rest area.

alsoKnownAs.title: Highlights
items: The ~3.5 km 'Song Hae Trail' (송해둘레길) loop is flat, free, and Dalseong-gun's most recognizable waterside space
Named after national MC Song Hae, with a memorial statue and photo zone carrying generations of Korean memory
Okyeonji lakeside ecology — water on three sides, with waterbirds, morning mist, and lake sunrise
Four-season flower fields in turn: spring azaleas and rapeseed, autumn cosmos and pink muhly — an open-air flower classroom
Free and open 24 hours, managed by Dalseong-gun, visitable anytime

transportSection.title: Transport & Arrival Guide
transportSection.subtitle: Consolidating outbound travel to Daegu, in-city transfers, walking/cycling, parking, and charging info for a clearer Songhae Park plan.
transportSection.overviewText: Songhae Park lies on the shore of Okyeonji Pond (옥연지) in Dalseong-gun, Daegu, Korea — Daegu's most recognizable lakeside park near the city. The easiest outbound option is KTX high-speed rail to Daegu (Dongdaegu Station) from Seoul or Busan, or a long-distance bus to Daegu; once in Dalseong-gun, a city bus or taxi from Okpo (옥포) takes about 10–20 minutes. Wrapping around the lake, you can enter the loop trail right from the drop-off point.
transportSection.overviewText2: The area around Songhae Park is lakeside green space with supporting parking. Plan transport, parking, and walking together — especially with seniors, young children, or luggage, parking then walking greatly reduces hassle.
arrivalItems: The park is on the Okyeonji shore in Okpo-eup, Dalseong-gun; about a 10–20 min walk from the Okpo neighborhood to the entrance.
The park is free 24 hours, but night lighting is limited; daytime or dusk visits are more comfortable.
Weekends and clear days draw crowds; avoid peak times. Parking fills easily — arrive early.

modes.label: KTX / Long-distance bus (to Daegu)
modes.title: Outbound to Daegu
modes.description: Easiest for most travelers: KTX from Seoul or Busan to Daegu (Dongdaegu Station), or a long-distance bus to Daegu Intercity Bus Terminal — the classic way to Songhae Park.
details: KTX from Seoul to Daegu about 1.5–2 hours (by train).
Then metro or taxi to Okpo, Dalseong-gun, about 40–60 minutes.
Use a T-money / transit card; metro, bus, and taxi connect easily.

steps.t: Take KTX or long-distance bus to Daegu.
Transfer to metro or taxi to Okpo.
Walk to the lakeside entrance of Songhae Park.

modes.label: Metro + city bus (to Okpo)
modes.title: In-city to the park
modes.description: Flexible and convenient: take the Daegu metro to Okpo (옥포), then a city bus or taxi to Songhae Park.
details: Metro from Dongdaegu to Okpo about 40–60 minutes.
Then bus or taxi to the park about 10–20 minutes.
Buses take T-money; use a map app for real-time arrivals.

steps.t: Transfer to bus or taxi to the entrance.
Enter the park along the loop trail.

modes.label: Okpo side (walk + shuttle)
modes.title: The hidden nearby option
modes.description: Okpo is the closest neighborhood to Songhae Park; a short shuttle bus or taxi from the station takes about 10–20 minutes — ideal for light packers who want a first look.
details: Frequent buses in Okpo; closest to the park.
About 10–20 minutes from the station to the main sights.
Mornings and weekdays are best to avoid transfers and crowds.

steps.t: Get off at Okpo by bus.
Transfer to bus or taxi, about 10–20 min.
Reach the loop trail and flower fields.

modes.label: Driving (parking / charging)
modes.title: Via Okyeonji-ro · nearby parking
modes.description: Good when traveling with seniors/children, lots of luggage, or touring Daegu; the park has supporting parking (mostly paid).
details: Set navigation to '송해공원' or address '대구 달성군 옥포읍 기세리 306' (306 Gise-ri, Okpo-eup, Dalseong-gun, Daegu).
Plenty of spaces, but fills on weekends and holidays.
Some public lots have EV chargers; drive slowly and park properly near the busy lake.

steps.t: Park in the lot, then walk in.
Avoid the 11:00–19:00 peak to save parking time.

modes.label: Taxi / ride-hailing
modes.title: Door-to-door
modes.description: Most convenient with luggage, seniors/children, or late arrivals.
details: Taxi from Daegu Station or downtown to Songhae Park about 40–60 minutes, metered.
You'll walk in from the park vicinity.
Taxis queue in peak and rainy seasons; reserve via a local app like Kakao T.

steps.t: Call a car via Kakao T or similar.
Get off near the park and walk in.

modes.label: Walk (lakeside greenway)
modes.title: From Okpo to the park
modes.description: If you're already in Okpo or on Okyeonji-ro, walking is the most natural way to observe the lakeside greenway and flower fields.
details: About a 10–20 minute walk from Okpo to the park.
Pass lawns, lake shore, and flower fields along the way.
Flat paths; wear comfortable shoes and watch children and belongings.

modes.label: Cycling / greenway walk
modes.title: Lakeside slow travel
modes.description: The most relaxing way to feel Okyeonji and the flower fields.
details: Flat along the loop road; walk to connect trail, statue, and flower fields.
Park bikes at designated racks; don't ride into wetlands or crowded areas.
About 10–20 minutes from Okpo to the park, with greenery throughout.

parking.title: Parking & Charging Overview
parking.note: Songhae Park has supporting parking (mostly paid). Below are the nearest main options; rates and availability vary by season and time — please follow on-site signs.

options.name: Songhae Park lot
options.price: Paid public parking, fills in peak season

options.name: Okpo neighborhood parking
options.price: Public / paid, more spaces but tight in peak

options.name: Nearby street parking
options.price: Roadside / small lots, few spaces, easier on weekdays

parking.disclaimer: Roads near the park congest on holidays and clear days; don't occupy bus or fire lanes for long. EV chargers are mostly in public lots; rates and limits may change — check posted signs.

driverFaq.q: Is there parking near Songhae Park?
driverFaq.a: The park has supporting parking (mostly paid), within walking distance. It fills easily on weekends and holidays — arrive early or prefer public transport.
The Songhae Park lot is about 50–200 m away, closest to the entrance; Okpo neighborhood parking is about 300–800 m, more spaces but tight in peak.
Little. Roads are narrow and congested on holidays; don't park roadside long — use proper lots and public transport.
Unless parking is essential, no. Weekends and clear days congest; walking or public transport is smoother. If driving, park then walk in.
Strongly. After KTX or bus to Daegu, transfer to Okpo by metro, then walk or taxi about 10–20 min to the park. Address: 306 Gise-ri, Okpo-eup, Dalseong-gun, Daegu, Korea.
For stability and ease, public transport remains optimal: KTX/bus to Daegu, then transfer. If driving is unavoidable, treat parking and shuttle as part of the trip, not 'drive to the door'.

localTips: Mornings and dusk remain golden; set arrival about 60 minutes before sunset for the best flower-and-statue reflections on the lake.
Weekends and clear days draw crowds; with kids or gear, allow buffer and avoid peaks.
Best pair Songhae Park with the Okyeonji loop and Song Hae statue; a single stop underestimates its lake-and-flower value.

bestTime.title: Best time to arrive
bestTime.text: Songhae Park is reachable 24 hours, but what truly sets the photo ceiling is the dawn and dusk light windows. Arrive about 60 minutes before sunset; if weather isn't good for photos, shift focus to the loop trail or statue views.

officialManagement.title: About this lakeside park
officialManagement.text: Songhae Park lies on the shore of Okyeonji Pond (옥연지) in Dalseong-gun, Daegu, Korea — a citizen's lakeside park transformed from former lakeside greenery and waterfront facilities, maintained and operated by Dalseong-gun as public space. It has long been a waterfront living room shared by residents strolling, travelers visiting, and families, and is one of Dalseong-gun's city cards of 'lake, flowers, and memory'.

humanities.title: People & Place: From Song Hae to Okyeonji
humanities.subtitle: Putting the life of Korea's national MC Song Hae, the toponym memory of Okyeonji, the modern design of the loop trail, and the four-season flower fields on one timeline is how you truly understand why this park is more than 'a pretty lake'.

sections.title: Song Hae the person: life of a national MC
sections.content: Song Hae (송해, born Song Byeong-yeop, 1926–2022) was Korea's most representative comedian, singer, and host. From 1988 he hosted KBS's National Singing Contest (전국노래자랑) for over thirty years, becoming the 'national grandfather' of generations of Koreans with his warm smile and the line 'sing your heart out.' In his later years he was made an honorary citizen of Dalseong-gun; naming the park after him is the locality's plain tribute to an artist who accompanied the nation for decades.

sections.title: The name 'Okyeonji' & toponym memory
sections.content: 'Okyeon' (옥연) means 'a deep, jade-like pond.' Okyeonji Pond (옥연지) is a natural lakeside body in Okpo, Dalseong-gun, whose name carries the locality's long memory of this clear water. Named 'Okyeonji Songhae Park,' toponym, water, and person interlock on the same green space.

sections.title: Birth of the park (2016–)
sections.content: Songhae Park began formal upgrades to Okyeonji's waterfront facilities in 2016, and from 2020 onward successively added four-season theme gardens, lakeside boardwalks (데크길), a memorial statue of Song Hae, and multiple photo zones, gradually becoming Dalseong-gun's representative free lakeside park, maintained by Dalseong-gun.

sections.title: Four-season flower fields & flower culture
sections.content: The park is known for seasonal flowers: spring azaleas (진달래) and rapeseed bloom first, summer greens the pond, and autumn brings cosmos (코스모스), pink muhly (핑크뮬리), and silver grass. The flower fields are not just scenery but an open-air flower classroom on Daegu's outskirts.

sections.title: Song Hae Trail (loop trail)
sections.content: The 'Song Hae Trail' (송해둘레길) built along Okyeonji is about 3.5 km — the park's most iconic modern facility. Flat and free, it connects the statue, flower fields, wetlands, and lakefront railings in a loop you can walk without backtracking, the main axis for daily strolls and visitor photos.

sections.title: Song Hae memorial statue & memorial space
sections.content: The park has a memorial statue and photo zone for Song Hae (송해), honoring the songs and smiles he shared with Koreans for decades. The statue is both the central photo landmark and a way the 'person–place–memory' relationship keeps being told in the park.

sections.title: The legend of Okyeonji (local oral tradition)
sections.content: About Okyeonji, local oral stories tie to a 'spirit of the deep pond': it is said the clear, deep water was long regarded as a sacred place, and in drought years people prayed for rain, believing a guardian spirit lived in the pond. Such legends may not appear in official histories, but they let the public sense how this land was imagined and cherished — exactly the value a non-profit science guide should amplify.

sections.title: Lake ecology of Okyeonji (science perspective)
sections.content: Okyeonji is a typical peri-urban still-water body. Shore vegetation, wetlands, and open water together form habitat for waterbirds and insects; seasonal water levels and vegetation shape different lake appearances. From base to observation below, understand how this lake became an urban green kidney with both scenic and educational value.

cards.title: Water body base
cards.text: Okyeonji is a relatively closed still-water lake, with levels tied to groundwater and rainfall. Stable water lets shore vegetation and wetlands develop into a continuous green belt.

cards.title: Shaping process
cards.text: The shoreline formed not at once but by long-term sediment accumulation, vegetation stabilizing banks, and water-level fluctuation. Gentle slopes and boardwalks let people approach the water without harming ecology.

cards.title: On-site observation
cards.text: Observe together with flower fields, wetlands, and lakefront railings. Reading alongside official signs makes it easier to understand how the lake and surrounding greenery determine the park's ecological form.

fieldNote.title: Observation tip
fieldNote.text: First take in Okyeonji's overall outline from a high lakeside point, then return to the trail to observe shore vegetation and wetlands up close. Distance views show overall form; close views reveal the ecological mechanism.

academicNote.title: Academic note
academicNote.text: This section is a science overview based on public park interpretation and on-site features. For stricter hydrological and ecological classification, rely on official materials, on-site signs, and academic research.

didYouKnow.title: Did you know?
didYouKnow.text: Songhae Park is more than lakeside greenery — it is an open-air classroom of personal memory and urban ecology: from the life of national MC Song Hae and the toponym of Okyeonji, to the 3.5 km Song Hae Trail and four-season flower fields, the story of land and people is written on the same shore of Okyeonji.

sitePlaques.title: Reading the On-site Signs & Interpretation
sitePlaques.subtitle: When you visit Songhae Park, what's worth reading slowly is often not the check-in board but the official signs explaining 'why this water is here'.
sitePlaques.intro: The readings below are based on Dalseong-gun's Okyeonji, Song Hae Trail, flower-field, statue, toponym, and ecology signage, translating information visible on-site but not always read into accessible English science notes.

items.title: Songhae Park & Okyeonji
items.body: These signs usually state the key background: the meaning of Songhae Park as an Okyeonji lakeside park, and its naming relationship with national MC Song Hae. Reading the hints is lesson one in using this lakeside landmark.

items.title: Song Hae Trail (loop)
items.body: The guide repeats the trail's status as a loop landmark and reminds visitors: half its charm is the open lakefront view, half the real water and flower shadows at your feet. The signs explain clearly 'why a loop trail'.

items.title: Four-season flower fields
items.body: The map explains 'why this is a flower hotspot': Okyeonji's terrain and soil let seasonal flowers bloom in turns. Seen with the loop trail, the park's design logic is clear — personal memory and citizen recreation coexist.

items.title: Song Hae memorial statue
items.body: Erected by Dalseong-gun, it marks the memorial relationship between the park and Song Hae (송해), echoing the 'national MC' motif. It reminds every visitor: this green belt connects Koreans' warmest collective memory with the most transparent lakeside experience.

gallery.title: Photo Gallery
gallery.subtitle: From the light on Okyeonji, the loop boardwalk of Song Hae Trail, to the colors of four-season flower fields — see the visual beauty of Songhae Park.

reviews.title: Visitor Reviews
reviews.declaration: Visitor feedback is available on Google Maps (external link).
reviews.featuredSubtitle: A few short lines that capture the spirit of Songhae Park.

featured.text: Walking the 3.5 km Song Hae Trail slowly, with Okyeonji's ripples at your feet — that lakeside calm is special, and at dusk the light makes it feel like floating on the water.
A free and open lakeside park, steps from the Song Hae statue — Daegu's most underrated corner near the city.
Walking the loop trail with my child, he watched the flower fields and listened to Grandpa Song Hae's stories; even my parents walked easily.

items.name: Minjun
items.text: Visited at dawn; the loop trail backlit is so photogenic, and the moment by the lake was completely silent — strongly recommend sunrise, best light.

items.name: Seoyeon
items.text: The 3.5 km Song Hae Trail composition is healing; about 15 min from Okpo, lake breeze is strong so dress warm.

items.name: Jihun
items.text: Worth it as free public space; weekends are crowded — weekdays or mornings are more comfortable.

items.name: Ha-eun
items.text: About 15 min by bus from Okpo to the park; the lakeside and flower fields along the way are pleasant for a half-day stroll.

mapSection.title: How to Get There

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.text: So that every traveler who comes to Daegu won't miss the lake and flower fields on the shore of Okyeonji, we have gathered scattered official information into this guide — to give you the purest experience of 'by the lake, among the flowers, reading a life'.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Practical information about Songhae Park's facilities, history, and visit planning.

items.q: Is there parking, and what does it cost?
items.a: The park has supporting parking (mostly paid), within walking distance. It fills easily on weekends and holidays — arrive early or prefer public transport.
Songhae Park has flat loop trails; wheelchairs and strollers can reach most viewpoints via the main path. But the lake side is windy and boardwalks slippery — stay on hard ground with company.

items.q: Are there restrooms or food inside?
items.a: As open lakeside greenery, restrooms and snacks concentrate at the entrance rest area and nearby; resupply water and food there before entering.

items.q: Is there fuel or EV charging nearby?
items.a: Public lots have EV chargers; traditional gas stations line Daegu city and Dalseong-gun — self-drivers can refuel on the way into the city.

items.q: What is the connection between 'Songhae Park' and Song Hae?
items.a: Song Hae (송해, 1926–2022) was Korea's national MC, hosting KBS's National Singing Contest for decades. Made an honorary citizen of Dalseong-gun, the park is named after him to honor the songs and smiles he shared with Koreans for years.

items.q: What is its special natural and cultural value?
items.a: Songhae Park is not a themed amusement park but a public waterfront space turning Okyeonji's still-water lake into personal memory, four-season flowers, loop trails, and wetland ecology. Flower fields, statue, and lake form a low-impact, high-empathy design — one of Dalseong-gun's city cards of 'lake and memory'.
Songhae Park itself is free and open 24 hours, with no gate, ticket, or reservation needed — visit anytime (please respect the public environment and avoid late-night noise).

items.q: How long does a visit take?
items.a: A relaxed walk takes about 1–2 hours (including trail and photo stops); allow half a day if you also visit the statue, flower fields, and Okpo neighborhood.

items.q: Can I go in bad weather?
items.a: Yes — the park is open space, visitable in any weather. But boardwalks are slippery and the lake breeze strong in rain; take wind and slip precautions, wear non-slip shoes, and watch the weather.

items.q: What else is worth visiting nearby?
items.a: From Songhae Park you can link the Okyeonji loop, Song Hae statue, Okpo-eup neighborhood, and other Dalseong-gun sights into a half-day 'lake—flowers—statue—greenery' waterfront route.

scienceArt.title: Park & City: Lake, Flowers & the National MC
scienceArt.subtitle: Dig below the surface 'pretty' to find what's truly rare about this park: it is at once public lakeside greenery, a personal memory, and an open-air urban waterfront classroom.

scienceMuseum.name: Song Hae & Dalseong-gun's memory
scienceMuseum.role: The personal story behind the statue
scienceMuseum.highlight: The hardest core of Songhae Park is both visible and invisible. Visible are the loop trail and Okyeonji's lake views; invisible is the 'national MC' narrative and Koreans' collective memory. Visitors see landscape; nostalgics see Song Hae placed on this lakeshore as a local text.
points: Core: personal memory, flower symbols, and urban geography light up this shore together.
Key: public lakefront facilities turned into a low-impact, high-empathy memory classroom.
Meaning: it upgrades the 'urban green kidney' into a memory template the public can pilgrimage to.

artMuseum.name: Seasonal symbols in the flower fields
artMuseum.highlight: Flower fields, loop trail, and Song Hae statue, together with Okyeonji, form Songhae Park's identity system: instantly reading as Dalseong-gun, as Okyeonji, and as a gentle, transparent waterfront aesthetic. From lake views to golden flower light, this contrast makes it one of Daegu's most memorable urban images.
points: Imagery: lake, flowers, statue form a strong identity.
Status: one of Daegu's most photogenic lakeside parks.
Narrative: it translates the personal theme into a publicly felt aesthetic.

outdoorMuseum.title: Photography & Photo Guide: Songhae Park Spots
outdoorMuseum.subtitle: As Daegu's most recognizable lakeside park, a few structured spots and times greatly improve your photos' usefulness and beauty.

items.name: Loop trail · lakefront view
items.background: From dawn to dusk, Okyeonji's ripples at your feet make the classic 'trail—lake' composition; railings silhouette beautifully backlit.
points: Use the trail as a leading line toward the lake.
Crouch low to layer trail and water for a steadier frame.

items.name: Lakefront railings · flower overlook
items.background: From the lakefront side, frame 'trail + Okyeonji + flower fields' together — Songhae Park's most recognizable spot.
points: Side light on railings at dawn is softest.
Watch your step and crowds; don't enter closed areas for composition.

items.name: Four-season flower fields
items.background: The flower fields' colors are the park's most atmospheric window; paired with the Song Hae statue, morning light on flowers sparks the imagination.
points: Shoot in blue hour; sky and lake colors balance best.
Leave large warm sky with flower-field silhouettes.

items.name: Night lights
items.background: After dark, railings and trail lights glow; Songhae Park reflects a river of lights — ideal for closing long-exposure night shots and lakeside portraits.
points: Use lights as a leading line toward the distance.
Raise ISO or use stabilization for night frames.

education.title: Why is this Dalseong-gun's lakeside landmark?
education.subtitle: What's most worth learning about Songhae Park isn't 'it got prettier' but how it re-integrated Okyeonji's still-water lake into the public's personal memory while keeping reverence for nature.

points.title: A lakeside living room still used by citizens
points.text: Songhae Park isn't a 'hide the lake and done' case, but a model that activates personal memory through public-space design and turns it into shared place.
items: Citizens give daily strolls and pilgrimages to this water and flowers.
Public space and the urban lakefront coexist long-term.

points.title: Writing memory ethics into visitor behavior
points.text: Signs, loop trail, and guide systems aren't just navigation but let every visitor, while using the space, casually respect public environment and others.
items: Visitors are guided to stay on low-impact paths.
Viewing experience and public life don't sacrifice each other.

points.title: Translating personal memory into public aesthetics
points.text: Songhae Park didn't erase the personal background but, through statue, flowers, and signage, lets the public sense what this land has been through while visiting.
items: Story depth and viewing experience complete in one place.
Exactly the value a non-profit science site should amplify.

plantGuide.title: Okyeonji's Four-Season Flower Calendar
plantGuide.subtitle: Dig below 'pretty flowers' to find what's truly rare about Songhae Park: it turns a peri-urban lake into an open-air flower classroom that changes with the seasons.

comparison.name: Azalea & Rapeseed
comparison.summary: Spring is the first season to wake in Songhae Park. Azaleas (진달래) blanket trails and slopes, with golden rapeseed, forming Daegu's brightest lakeside palette.
points: Bloom roughly March–April as temperatures rise.
Azaleas are low and mass-planted — the base color of the loop trail.
Paired with lake reflections, most springtime energetic.

comparison.name: Cosmos & Pink Muhly
comparison.summary: Autumn is the park's most popular season. Cosmos (코스모스) ripple in the wind, pink muhly (핑크뮬리) hazes into pink mist, and silver grass lights the shore.
points: Bloom roughly September–November, peak in crisp autumn.
Pink muhly best shot backlit at dusk.
With statue and lake, a warm autumn image.

plantGuide.seasonSubtitle: One lakeshore, four tempers. Below, the flowers and scenes most worth expecting each season.

seasons.name: Spring · Azalea & Rapeseed
seasons.summary: As it warms, azaleas and rapeseed lead the shore — the best season for family outings and morning shots.
highlights: Golden rapeseed against green lake, strong contrast.
Calm lake at dawn, clearest reflections.

seasons.name: Summer · Green Pond
seasons.summary: Midsummer greens are lushest; shaded lake-side trees make it a cool stroll and good for waterbirds.
highlights: Loop trail shaded, more comfortable feel.
Avoid midday sun; mornings and dusk.
Wetlands active with waterbirds, good for slow watching.

seasons.name: Autumn · Cosmos & Pink Muhly
seasons.summary: The year's most popular season. Cosmos, pink muhly, and silver grass take turns, dyeing the shore a pink-gold palette.
highlights: Softest dusk light, highest photo rate.

seasons.name: Winter · Quiet Shore
seasons.summary: After foliage falls, the shore is more open and quiet — a crisp season for morning mist and lake sunrise.
highlights: Morning mist over the lake, distant and pure.
Clear-day sunrise gold on water, best for slow shots.
Dress warm and mind the lakefront wind.

audience.title: Who Should Come? Upgrade Segment Guides to Custom Itineraries
audience.subtitle: Not just 'you'll like it,' but directly telling you how to walk, where to go first, and which Daegu nodes to link.

items.resonance: Free, open, flat loop trail — kids can watch flower fields, hear Grandpa Song Hae's stories, see the lake, and easily reach the statue along the flat side.
items.tip: Spend energy on photo stops, not on crowding.

items.resonance: Backlit flower fields at dawn are Daegu's most romantic frames, with very high hit rate.
items.tip: Count arrival, return, and light into the plan so composition isn't beaten by on-site pace.

items.resonance: As a personal-memory sample, Song Hae's life, trail design, and Okyeonji ecology are worth a close look.
items.tip: Avoid the most crowded weekends; choose dawn or a weekday afternoon to really observe details.

items.resonance: Without going far, experience Korean personal culture and urban lake ecology near Daegu, while linking metro, buses, and local food — an ideal start to Dalseong-gun's 'lake and memory' character.
items.tip: If you can only pick one Daegu outskirts landmark, Songhae Park best opens the 'person and city' theme.

lodging.title: Lodging Guide: Stay Close, or Stay Convenient
lodging.subtitle: Daegu is a city where 'industry, education, and lakes' coexist. We don't recommend specific hotels but help you parse two lodging patterns to choose what fits.

options.name: Dalseong-gun / Okpo area
options.highlight: Staying in Dalseong-gun or Okpo (옥포) puts you a short drive or walk from Songhae Park, Okyeonji, and flower fields; the lake breeze and sunrise at dawn suit travelers best. Ideal for those focused on 'lake stroll + flowers' with high convenience needs.
options.commute: Commute: to the park about 10–20 min walk or bus. Walking is easy on the legs, good for dawn lakeside.
points: Loop trail 10–20 min by walk or bus, least effort.
Lake, snacks, and flower fields extremely rich.
More choices, usually better value.

options.name: Daegu city / Dongdaegu area
options.highlight: Staying in Daegu city or Dongdaegu (동대구) puts high-speed rail, commercial districts, and food streets at your door — ideal for 'lake + transfer' travelers who head to Songhae Park by metro or car by day.
options.commute: Commute: metro to Okpo about 40–60 minutes. Good for self-drivers or independent travelers wanting absolute convenience.
points: High-speed hub and districts at hand, good for transfers.
Food, cafes, night markets extremely rich.
To Songhae Park by metro or short drive about 40–60 minutes.

lodging.peak: Daegu's spring/autumn flower seasons and clear weekends tighten rooms and raise prices as tourists flood in. Book weeks ahead; if booking near holidays, expand the range to nearby Gyeongju or Busan and travel by train or car.
tips: For sunrise and flower shots: prefer Dalseong-gun / Okpo, walk to the park at dawn.
For convenience and food: choose Daegu city / Dongdaegu, walk and dine at hand.
Before booking, confirm breakfast, parking, and EV chargers (key for self-drivers).
Peak seasons and holidays need advance planning to avoid no rooms or high prices.
